full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Checking the exact rules so I don't lose marks.
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: Can you read my exam instructions and tell me the strict rules for picking a paper? I need the exact allowed years and topics.
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: It gave me a quick checklist of the rules so I wouldn't get disqualified for picking the wrong year or topic.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I double-checked the AI's summary against the official Midsem PDF to make sure it didn't miss anything.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Finding a paper that I can actually code on my laptop.
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: I need a paper on Support Vector Machines from 2009 to 2012. Can you suggest one that is easy to code on my laptop and doesn't have crazy math?
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: It suggested 'Training SVMs Without Offset' and explained that the math is simple because it just removes the b term.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I checked the JMLR website to confirm the paper was real and actually published in 2011.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Understanding the main idea of the paper.
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: I'm looking at 'Training SVMs Without Offset'. Can you explain in simple words what 'offset' means and why removing it makes the model faster?
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: It explained the geometry of the line and how removing the offset makes the math much faster to calculate.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I read the introduction of the paper to make sure the AI's explanation matched what the authors were actually saying.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Asking a doubt about how the math actually works in real life.
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: I have a doubt. If we remove 'b', the line has to go exactly through zero. What if the data points are grouped far away from zero? Won't it fail to separate them?
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: It explained that while this is a problem in 2D, in high dimensions (like with kernels) the data is spread out enough that a line through zero works perfectly.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I drew a 2D graph to see the problem myself, then read the paper's section on high dimensions to understand the solution.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Making sure I didn't pick the wrong topic.
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: My friend suggested a Gaussian Mixture Model paper instead. Is this SVM paper easier to code and explain than a GMM paper, since this choice will also affect my Parts B and C?
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: It helped me realize that coding GMM math from scratch is very hard, and this SVM paper is a much safer choice for my exam.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I remembered our class lectures on GMMs and agreed that the math would be too hard to code from scratch on a deadline.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Checking if the math in this 64-page paper is too hard for me.
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: This paper has 64 pages, which is very long. Is the math too complex for me to code, or is the main idea simple enough for my exam?
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: It reassured me that most of the pages are just heavy proofs for the journal, and the main coding part is actually very small and simple.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I skimmed through the PDF and saw that the actual experiment setup is clear and the rest is just theory I don't need to code.

top_5_prompts.prompt: I have a doubt. If we remove 'b', the line has to go exactly through zero. What if the data points are grouped far away from zero? Won't it fail to separate them?
top_5_prompts.why_important: This was my biggest conceptual doubt. I couldn't visualize how a model stuck at zero could work. Asking this helped me understand high-dimensional geometry, which is great for my Part C viva.

top_5_prompts.prompt: I need a paper on Support Vector Machines from 2009 to 2012. Can you suggest one that is easy to code on my laptop and doesn't have crazy math?
top_5_prompts.why_important: This was the most important search question. It focused on my real limits (laptop hardware and math skills) and helped me find the perfect, practical choice.

top_5_prompts.prompt: My friend suggested a Gaussian Mixture Model paper instead. Is this SVM paper easier to code and explain than a GMM paper, since this choice will also affect my Parts B and C?
top_5_prompts.why_important: This helped me weigh my options. It stopped me from picking a mathematically heavy GMM paper that would have caused me to struggle in Part B.

top_5_prompts.prompt: I'm looking at 'Training SVMs Without Offset'. Can you explain in simple words what 'offset' means and why removing it makes the model faster?
top_5_prompts.why_important: This helped me deeply understand the paper's main idea in simple English, which is exactly what I need to confidently explain it to the teacher.

top_5_prompts.prompt: Can you read my exam instructions and tell me the strict rules for picking a paper? I need the exact allowed years and topics.
top_5_prompts.why_important: This was the foundation. By asking for a summary of the strict rules first, I made sure I would not get disqualified for a silly mistake.
